coredata.dc:title: Photothermal enhanced biosensing platform of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac for dopamine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhaofei
Wang, Xinyue
Wei, Yuhang
Xie, Weiwei
Li, Song
Chen, Zhiming
coredata.dc:description: As a biomarker of endocrine gland diseases, the rapid and accurate determination of dopamine has a great significance. The aim of this study was to improve the dopamine biosensing performance of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac (laccase immobilized on Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites) by using the photothermal effect of magnetic carriers. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements revealed that the Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites enhanced the electron transfer between laccase and the electrode. The Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor presented an ultra-fast response to substrates, accurately detected dopamine within 0.2 s. Dopamine biosensing assays demonstrated that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor possessed high sensitivity, remarkable tolerances towards organic compounds and metal ions, and excellent selectivity and stability. Amperometric curves indicated that the illumination brought a higher current to the biosensor compared to the dark environment. The Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor held a low detection limit of 0.79 μM (S/N = 3) and a broad linear range of 1–1000 μM under the irradiation. Most significantly, the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor provided reliable results in the photothermal analysis of dopamine in synthetic urine samples. This finding paves the way for using the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor for photothermal detection of dopamine in biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photothermal biosensor
Immobilized laccase
Dopamine
Rapid and sensitive
Amperometric detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tolerances, selectivity and stability of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor
Photothermal detection of dopamine using Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor
Photothermal determination of dopamine in synthetic urine samples using Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Major Project of Anhui Province Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac and photothermal catalytic oxidation of dopamine on the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites.
HAADF-STEM image and corresponding element mapping of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites.

(a) CV curves and (b) Nyquist plots of GCE, Fe3O4@CS-Au and Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac electrodes in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, (c) CV curves of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac electrode at different pH for 200 μM dopamine, and (d) the relationship between oxidation peak potentials and pH values. Scan rate: 100 mV s−1.

(a) CV responses of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor to 200 μM dopamine at different scan rates of 10–100 mV s−1 and (b) linear plot for anodic and cathodic peak current response of dopamine vs. square root of scan rate.

Amperometric responses of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensors to successive additions of (a) 10 μM dopamine, and 10 μL Eth, TCM, Met, Ace, DMSO and DMF, (b) 10 μM dopamine, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, and Mg2+, and (c) 10 μM dopamine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, and glucose into 20 mL acetate buffer solution (pH = 4.5, 0.2 M). (d) Stability of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor after scanning in 0.2 M acetate buffer solution with 200 μM dopamine for 50 cycles.

(a) amperometric responses of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor to successive additions of dopamine, and (b) linear relationship between peak currents and dopamine concentrations. Light source: 980 nm laser, 1 W·cm−2.
Detection results for dopamine in synthetic urine samples.

head.ce:title: Photothermal enhanced biosensing platform of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac for dopamine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering
Anhui Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu 241000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biology and Food Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biology and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biology and Food Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu 241000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a biomarker of endocrine gland diseases, the rapid and accurate determination of dopamine has a great significance. The aim of this study was to improve the dopamine biosensing performance of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac (laccase immobilized on Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites) by using the photothermal effect of magnetic carriers. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements revealed that the Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites enhanced the electron transfer between laccase and the electrode. The Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor presented an ultra-fast response to substrates, accurately detected dopamine within 0.2 s. Dopamine biosensing assays demonstrated that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor possessed high sensitivity, remarkable tolerances towards organic compounds and metal ions, and excellent selectivity and stability. Amperometric curves indicated that the illumination brought a higher current to the biosensor compared to the dark environment. The Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor held a low detection limit of 0.79 μM (S/N = 3) and a broad linear range of 1–1000 μM under the irradiation. Most significantly, the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor provided reliable results in the photothermal analysis of dopamine in synthetic urine samples. This finding paves the way for using the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor for photothermal detection of dopamine in biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Early diagnosis of diseases is conducive to early prevention and treatment, reducing disease progression and reducing mortality [1]. Numerous nanomaterials and analytical methods have been proposed for the diagnosis and therapy of early diseases [2–8]. Dopamine is an important neurotransmitter that plays crucial functions in the physiological process of mammalian central nervous systems [9,10]. It is also recognized as a clinically significant biomarker for endocrine gland disorders, including multiple endocrine neoplasms II, pheochromocytoma, and medullary carcinoma of the thyroid [11,12]. Consequently, the rapid determination of dopamine is strongly required in early diagnosis and monitoring of these disorders. Thanks to many years of efforts from various groups, various detection techniques, including chromatography [13–15], chemiluminescence [16,17], spectrophotometry [16,18] and electrochemical biosensing [19–28], have been developed for the biomedical diagnosis and treatment, in which laccase based electrochemical biosensing has increasingly received more attention as it is convenient, inexpensive, sensitive and highly selective [10,18,29].

Laccase, a well-known multicopper oxidase, is an industrial enzyme of high economic value due to its extensive application in the textile, printing, and dyeing industries. It is widely used for decolorizing dyes, oxidizing organic pollutants, and developing biosensors [21,30–33]. However, their biosensing applications are inhibited due to the poor operational performance and low stability of free laccase [34,35]. Immobilizing laccase on electrode surface is an essential strategy to enhance the operational performance and stability of the biosensors [36,37]. Nonetheless, the immobilization process may reduce laccase activity, leading to a decrease in the sensitivity of the biosensor. Therefore, the synthesis of the immobilized laccase with a high catalytic activity is necessary for the biosensing application.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles are promising carriers due to their unique magnetic and optical properties [38,39]. It has been reported that Fe3O4-based nanoparticles can convert light energy into heat, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity of the immobilized laccase [31]. In this context, the sensitivity of the immobilized laccase biosensor can be improved by the illumination. Moreover, previous literatures revealed that the sensitivity of the biosensor was influenced by the conductivity of the magnetic carriers [14,40,41]. Based on the above, we synthesized the photothermal enhanced biosensing platform of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac for dopamine detection. Dopamine biosensing assays indicated that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor possessed the characteristics of a wide response range, low detection limit, high sensitivity, remarkable tolerances to organic compounds and metal ions, and good selectivity and stability. The immobilized laccase biosensing platform was demonstrated to be a rapid photothermal biosensor. Most importantly, the photothermal biosensor presented swift response to dopamine with a reliable result in the analysis of synthetic urine samples.

Crude laccase with catalytic activity about 12.1 U/mL was produced according to the previous method and used without any purification [42]. Chitosan (CS, viscosity ≤ 200 mPa.s) and mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Corporation. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylamino propyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All other chemicals were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation.

Initially, 2.0 mM FeCl3·6H2O, 1.0 mM FeCl2·4H2O, and 3.0 mL 28 % ammonia were dissolved in 35.0 mL water under Ar flow. The solution was stirred and heated to 65 °C for 1.5 h. Then, the temperature was increased to 93 °C and maintained for 1 h. After that, the obtained Fe3O4 nanoparticles were collected and thoroughly washed with deionized water. Subsequently, 100 mg Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed in 30 mL of 1.3 mg/mL chitosan solution. Upon adding 2 mL of glutaraldehyde (5 wt%), the mixture was incubated at 50 °C for 1 h. The precipitates (Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles) were separated and alternately washed with ethanol and deionized water. In the third stage, the obtained Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles were dispersed in 30 mL water. Subsequently, 20 μL HAuCl4 (23.5 wt%) was added and heated to boiling. Then, 2 mL of 1 % trisodium citrate solution was quickly added and refluxed for 30 min. The precipitates (Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites) were collected and alternately washed with ethanol and deionized water.

In a typical synthesis, 5 mg Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites were dispersed in 4 mL of 20 uM MPA solution. The mixture was incubated in the dark at 25 °C for 2 h. The resulting products (Fe3O4@CS-Au-MPA nanocomposites) were collected, washed with deionized water, and dispersed in 4 mL acetate buffer solution (pH 4.5). After introducing 40 mg of EDC and 60 mg of NHS, the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 0.5 h. The precipitates were collected, washed with deionized water, and dispersed in 1 mL water. Then, 1 mL crude laccase was added, and the immobilization of laccase was conducted at 30 °C for 1 h. Finally, the obtained Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac was collected, washed with deionized water, and stored at 4 °C for further use.

In brief, 5 μL of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac (5 mg/mL) was directly deposited on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and dried at 4 °C for subsequent electrochemical measurements. For comparison, the Fe3O4@CS-Au electrode was prepared using the same method.

Transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out on a JEOL JEM-2010F microscope (200 kV). The crystal structures of the magnetic samples were analyzed through X-ray diffraction (XRD), employing a Rigaku SmartLab(9) diffractometer. The surface of the magnetic samples was examined using a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer and a Thermo Nicolet iS20 spectrophotometer. Magnetic properties were investigated on a vibrating sample magnetometer (PPMS-9 T) at 300 K. All electrochemical measurements were meticulously performed on a Chenhua electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E) at a constant temperature of 25 °C.

Fig. 1 depicts the synthesis of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac for dopamine detection, which involved multi-step procedures: (1) fabrication of Fe3O4 nanoparticles as magnetic cores; (2) synthesis of Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles by coating CS shells on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles; (3) preparation of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites by reducing HAuCl4 on the surface of Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles; (4) functionalizing Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites with carboxyl groups by reacting with sulfydryl group of MPA; (5) immobilization of laccase by reacting with crude laccase using the EDC/NHS method. Fig. 2 a illustrates the typical low-magnification TEM image of the Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites. It was observed that Au nanocrystals were attached to Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles to form the Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites. Careful observation showed that the Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles exhibited a core-shelled structure with the inner Fe3O4 cores and the outer chitosan layer. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 2b disclosed the structural information of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites. The lattice spacing measured from the nanocrystal was 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (111) planes of the cubic Au structure. The 0.25 nm spacing of lattice fringes of the nanoparticles was attributed to the (311) crystallographic planes of cubic Fe3O4. The Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites were further investigated by the high-angle annular dark field scan TEM (HAADF-STEM) and elemental mapping analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 , the dark-field image of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites indicated numerous Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles interconnecting with each other, and Au nanocrystals connected to the Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles. Fe, O, and Au elements were displayed in the whole area of the sample, demonstrating the structure of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites.

Crystal structures of the magnetic samples were determined by XRD, and diffraction patterns of Fe3O4@CS and Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites are described in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S1a, all diffraction peaks of Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles can be indexed to the characteristic planes of cubic Fe3O4 (PDF 01–072-6170). The diffraction peaks of Fig. S1b were attributed to the mixture of cubic Fe3O4 and Au (PDF 00–004-0784), indicating the crystal purity of the Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites. Magnetic hysteresis loop was used to characterize the magnetic properties of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites, and exhibited in Fig. S2. The typical ferromagnetic curve gave a coercivity value of 45.9 Oe for Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites. The saturation magnetization was 36.8 emu/g, which was adequate for efficient magnetic separation from the solution by using a magnet. Fig. S3 describes the typical C 1 s, O 1 s, Fe 2p, and Au 4f XPS spectra of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites. The C 1 s spectrum of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites revealed four peaks at 284.4, 285.4, 286.1, and 288.4 eV corresponding to C atoms in CC, COC, CN, and CO bonds [43–45], respectively. The peaks located at 529.7, 531.3 and 533.0 eV in the O 1 s spectrum were assigned to O atoms in F-O, CO and COC bonds [46,47], respectively. Additionally, Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 at 710.4 and 724.1 eV were observed in the Fe 2p spectrum [47], respectively, verifying the formation of Fe3O4@CS nanoparticles. Concurrently, the peaks of Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 were found to be located at 83.6 and 87.2 eV, respectively, corresponding to reported values for the Au in metallic form [48]. These results demonstrated that the obtained samples are Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites.

FT-IR spectrum of the Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites was compared with the spectra of magnetic Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@CS-Au-MPA nanocomposites to justify the stepwise architecture, and listed in Fig. S4. The absorption bands at 557 and 431 cm−1 were consistent with the characteristic of the FeO bond of Fe3O4 [49,50]. Moreover, the absorption band at 3408 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of the –OH group indicated the presence of water molecules being adsorbed on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in all spectra [51]. These observations further confirmed the presence of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles in each step of the experiment. Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites were evidenced by new peaks appeared in absorption bands of stretching vibration of –NH2 at 3410–3450 cm−1, bands of –CH2- at 2924 and 2852 cm−1, –NH2 deformation vibration at 1616 cm−1 (overlapped with -CNH stretching vibration at 1635 cm−1), CO, CN and COC stretching at 1375, 1237 and 1055 cm−1, stretching at 1237 cm−1 [52–54], respectively. As the Fe3O4@CS-Au-MPA nanocomposites, a new peak appeared at 1725 cm−1, which corresponded to the carboxyl groups (–COO-) [55]. Also, the band at 700 cm−1 related to the C-S group was observed in the spectrum of Fe3O4@CS-Au-MPA nanocomposites [45]. These observations revealed that the carboxyl-functionalized Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites had been successfully prepared.

The electrochemical behavior of modified electrodes was meticulously examined using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. Fig. 4 a provided the CVs performances of GCE, Fe3O4@CS-Au and Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac electrodes showing redox couples of Fe2+/Fe3+ in the ferrocyanide complex. At the surface of the GCE, there was a pair of redox peaks characterized by an anodic peak current (Ipa) of 10.6 μA and a peak potential separation (ΔEp) of 0.133 V. After modifying the GCE surface with Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites, the signal strength was amplified, and ΔEp was reduced (Ipa = 13.3 μA, ΔEp = 0.101 V). This could be ascribed to the improved conductivity of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites. In the case of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac electrode, a decreased Ipa and an increased ΔEp were obtained (Ipa = 8.2 μA, ΔEp = 0.146 V). This was due to laccase molecules blocking the active sites for electron transfer, thereby reducing the redox peak current and increasing the peak separation [56].

The electrochemical behavior of biosensors was further validated using EIS tests to evaluate the charge transfer across the electrodes. Fig. 4b showed the EIS status of GCE, Fe3O4@CS-Au and Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac electrode surface. Compared with the Rct of ca. 129.4 Ω for the bare GCE, the Ret of Fe3O4@CS-Au electrode was reduced to ca. 112.9 Ω, implying a lower charge transfer resistance to Fe3O4@CS-Au electrode arising from the higher conductivity of the Au nanocrystals. The Rct value of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac electrode was increased to ca. 175.6 Ω, indicating the poor electrical conductivity resulting from laccase molecules immobilized on the Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites. These results suggested that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac electrode has been constructed successfully.

The optimum pH of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor for dopamine detection was investigated and presented in Fig. 4c. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the oxidation currents of the biosensor increase with increasing pH value until reaching a maximum at pH 4.5. Subsequently, the currents started to decrease as the pH value further increased within the range of 4.5 to 5.9. The oxidation peak potential displayed a linear relationship with the pH value (Fig. 4d), suggesting that protons played a significant role in the primary reaction process of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor, which involved the reduction of oxygen to water by the immobilized laccase.[54] The catalyzed reaction mechanism illustrated in Fig. 1 was described as follows:
(1)
Dopamine +
Lac ox
→
Dopamine - quinone
+
Lac red
+
2H
+
+ 2e
-
(2)
Lac red
+
1 2
O 2
+
2H
+
+ 2
e -
→
Lac ox
+
H
2
O

Kinetic study of electrode reactions can be used to predict the diffusion rate of electroactive compounds and is significant for the biosensor. Herein, the CVs of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor were performed in a 0.2 M acetate buffer solution (pH 4.5) containing 200 μM dopamine at different scan rates. Fig. 5 a displayed that the redox peak currents were intensified with increasing scan rates, and these currents were proportional to the square roots of scan rates. The fitting results presented in Fig. 5b demonstrated linear relationships for both the anodic and cathodic peak current plots versus scan rate (Ipa = 1.05 + 2.44v 1/2, R2 = 0.997; Ipc = -0.70–2.29v 1/2, R2 = 0.996), where Ipa, Ipc, and v represented the anodic peak currents (μA), cathodic peak currents (μA), and scan rates (mVs−1), respectively. These linear dependences implied that the electrochemical reaction at the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac electrode is controlled by the diffusion process [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tolerances, selectivity and stability of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In general, laccase biosensors can be disturbed by contaminants present in dopamine samples. To evaluate the tolerance performances of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensors, the inhibitory effect of organic solvents and metal ions on the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor was investigated by adding contaminants to the detection systems. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, when 10 μL ethanol (Eth), trichloromethane (TCM), methanol (Met), acetone (Ace), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were sequentially added into 20 mL of dopamine in acetate buffer solution (pH = 4.5, 0.2 M) with stirring, the current responses showed no significant change, indicating that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor exhibits exceptional tolerance towards organic compounds. The resistances of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor to metal ions were determined by sequentially adding 10 μM dopamine, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ into 20 mL acetate buffer solution (pH = 4.5, 0.2 M) with stirring.No significant change was observed in the current responses (Fig. 6b), suggesting no inhibition of these metals. The outstanding resistances of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor were attributed to the protection of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites, which subsequently enhances the laccase rigidity and tolerance towards the added contaminants [58].

In order to assess selectivity and anti-interference capabilities of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensors, 10 μM dopamine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, and glucose were sequentially added to 20 mL of acetate buffer solution (pH = 4.5, 0.2 M). As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor demonstrated a swift and sensitive response to the introduction of dopamine. In contrast, no obvious amperometric response was observed to the interference agents. Upon reintroducing dopamine, the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor continued to provide a sensitive and swift current response. These findings demonstrated that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor possessed good selectivity and anti-interference capabilities. Additionally, the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor has satisfactory stability. As described in Fig. 6d, the oxidation peak current of the CV after the 50 cycles had dropped ca. 7.04 %, and the reduction peak current decreased ca. 3.58 %. These excellent electrochemical properties indicated that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac is a reliable tool for rapid and sensitive detection of dopamine in practical settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal detection of dopamine using Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Amperometric responses to successive additions of dopamine on the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor under illumination and dark environment were compared and described in Fig. 7 a. The Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor presented a rapid current response to the added dopamine (within 0.2 s), demonstrating the practical applicability of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor. It should be mentioned that the illumination imparted a higher current to the biosensor compared to the dark environment. The higher current of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor was due to the photothermal conversion increasing the surface temperature of the Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac. Fig. 7b shows the linear dependence of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor on dopamine under illumination and dark environment. When the detection process was carried out under dark conditions, a detection range of 5 μM to 1000 μM with a limit of detection 4.45 μM (S/N = 3) was observed. In contrast, under the illumination, the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor possessed a wider detection range of 1–1000 μM with a lower detection limit of 0.79 μM (S/N = 3). The lower detection limit demonstrated the advantage of the photothermal detection of Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal determination of dopamine in synthetic urine samples using Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor was investigated by photothermal analysis of synthetic urine samples. Table 1 described the comparison of detection results of HPLC and Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor. It was observed that no dopamine was detected in the synthetic urine raw liquor by using HPLC or the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor. After the same concentration of dopamine was added, the results of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor were consistent with those of HPLC. The relative standard deviation (RSD) and recovery percentage for dopamine detection in synthetic urine samples by the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor were within the ranges of 0.70 %-6.36 % and 97.0 %-99.9 %, respectively. These results indicated that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor has promising applicability for photothermal detection of dopamine in synthetic urine samples.

In summary, Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites were synthesized and utilized for laccase immobilization. The optimum pH of the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor was observed at pH 4.5. Amperometric curves indicated that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor exhibited high sensitivity, remarkable tolerances towards organic compounds and metal ions, as well as excellent selectivity and stability. Due to the photothermal effect of Fe3O4@CS-Au nanocomposites, the immobilized laccase was demonstrated to be a rapid biosensor for the detection of dopamine. The Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor possesses the characteristics of a wide response range (1–1000 μM), and low detection limit (0.79 μM) under the irradiation. Most importantly, the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor provided reliable results in the photothermal analysis of synthetic urine samples. The exceptional biosensing performances indicate that the Fe3O4@CS-Au-Lac biosensor holds great potential for photothermal detection of dopamine in biomedical applications.

Zhaofei Liu: Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xinyue Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Yuhang Wei: Validation, Data curation. Weiwei Xie: Visualization, Investigation. Song Li: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Zhiming Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Major Project of Anhui Province Universities (2022AH040135), NaturalScienceResearchProjectforAnhuiUniversities (KJ2021A0509) and AnhuiNaturalScienceFoundation (2208085MC83).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110094.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
